name: Lam Research Corporation

profile.description: Lam Research designs, manufactures, and services semiconductor processing equipment used in the fabrication of integrated circuits. Nearly every advanced chip in the world is built with Lam technology, making the company a critical enabler of AI, advanced memory, and leading-edge foundry logic.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Innovation Track Record (2/3): Lam has developed breakthrough fabrication tools — SABRE 3D for advanced packaging and VECTOR TEOS 3D enabling dense memory interconnects for AI chips — while expanding from etch into deposition, clean, and advanced packaging [3][4]. AI Deployment (2/2): AI-driven innovation is embedded in product development — Lam uses virtual twins and AI to develop fabrication processes, and its equipment directly enables AI chip manufacturing with specific revenue from AI-related semiconductor spending [3]. Market Share (2/2): 'Nearly every advanced chip is built with Lam technology' — the company is a dominant leader in etch, deposition, and clean processes for advanced semiconductor fabrication [3]. Pipeline (2/2): Advanced packaging growing >40% in 2026, NAND inflection beginning, WFE market projected at $135B for 2026, new $65M Tualatin facility opening, and multi-year R&D partnership with CEA Leti [1][3]. R&D Intensity (1/1): $2.0B annual R&D spending (~12% of revenue) reflects top-tier investment for semiconductor equipment [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Lam positions itself as 'a fundamental enabler of the fourth industrial revolution' — specific, connecting semiconductor manufacturing to broader technological progress, and validated by 46 years of execution [3]. The mission is meaningful but shared broadly across the semiconductor ecosystem. Community Impact (1/2): $65M new Tualatin facility investment in the Silicon Forest, R&D partnerships with academic institutions (CEA Leti), and contribution to semiconductor workforce development [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): 19,700 employees with planned leadership transitions (new COO Varadarajan, Pat Lord retirement after 20+ years), values-based culture emphasis, and CEO Archer's tenure since 2018 [3]. Insider Buying (0/2): Massive net insider selling — CFO Bettinger sold $20.5M in March 2026 after option exercises, and Officer Harter sold $1.3M [6].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically calculated score reflects exceptional semiconductor equipment financials. D/E of 44.2% is conservative, ROE of 65.6% demonstrates capital-efficient operations, and profit margin of 30.2% significantly exceeds the 20% target [6]. Record TTM revenue of $20.6B grew 27% YoY with record gross margin of 49.9% [1]. The only weakness is the forward P/E of 33.1x, which remains above the 20x target but is justified by the AI-driven secular growth cycle. Dividend metrics are solid: 20.1% payout ratio provides substantial growth runway, with 11+ consecutive years paid and ~10% 5yr dividend CAGR [6].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Lam Research IS AI infrastructure — you cannot manufacture AI chips without Lam's etch, deposition, and clean equipment. Every advanced semiconductor fab in the world depends on Lam's tools, and AI's explosive growth directly increases demand for semiconductor fabrication capacity [1][3]. The company's equipment requires deep physics and materials science expertise, proprietary process recipes, and decades of customer relationships that cannot be replicated by software. Export controls and geopolitical factors further protect the competitive moat. AI Opportunity (1/1): Meaningful AI revenue — AI-driven semiconductor demand is the primary growth driver, with management specifically citing AI applications as the key catalyst for WFE spending reaching $135B in 2026 and advanced packaging growing >40% [1].

infinite_game.summary: Lam Research demonstrates solid infinite game characteristics through its enabling mission and collaborative approach to innovation partnerships, though its competitive language occasionally tilts toward market dominance framing.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): 'Fundamental enabler of the fourth industrial revolution' connects Lam's semiconductor equipment manufacturing to broader human progress, validated by 46 years of continuous innovation [3]. CEO Archer's statement about 'increasing velocity for the AI era' reflects genuine purpose alignment. However, the cause is shared with the broader semiconductor ecosystem and hasn't been uniquely differentiated.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Values-based culture is emphasized in corporate communications, and the planned succession of COO (Varadarajan replacing Pat Lord after 20+ years) demonstrates orderly leadership development [3]. CEO Archer's tenure since 2018 provides stability, and the 19,700 employees operate across a global footprint. However, no specific evidence of exceptional psychological safety or employee empowerment metrics.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Lam's partnership approach — adding Cadence CEO to the board, multi-year R&D collaboration with CEA Leti, and framing of competition as a shared semiconductor ecosystem challenge — reflects an infinite competitive mindset focused on collective advancement rather than defeating specific rivals [3]. Management discusses industry growth rather than market share battles.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Lam has evolved from a pure-play etch company to a comprehensive semiconductor equipment platform spanning deposition, clean, and advanced packaging [3]. The Novellus acquisition (2012) was transformative, and the current pivot to AI-era tools (SABRE 3D, VECTOR TEOS 3D) represents meaningful adaptation. However, the core business model — selling semiconductor fabrication equipment — has remained fundamentally the same.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Lam maintained $2.0B annual R&D investment through the FY2024 semiconductor equipment downturn (revenue declined to $14.9B from $17.4B), demonstrating willingness to invest counter-cyclically [1][6]. The $65M Tualatin facility investment during the recovery phase shows commitment to long-term capability building. However, these decisions align with industry norms for capital-intensive semiconductor equipment companies.

investment_thesis: Lam Research is one of the purest plays on AI semiconductor spending, with record TTM revenue of $20.6B (up 27% YoY), record gross margins of 49.9%, and Q2 FY2026 revenue hitting $5.34B — the 10th consecutive quarter of growth [1]. Q3 FY2026 guidance of $5.7B (±$300M) implies ~30% YoY growth acceleration, with management projecting WFE spending to reach $135B in 2026 driven by AI, advanced packaging (>40% growth), and NAND recovery [1]. The company's competitive position is nearly unassailable — 'nearly every advanced chip is built with Lam technology' — and the AI capex cycle is still in early innings, with major hyperscalers committing $150B+ to data center buildout in 2026-2027 [3]. At 30.2% profit margins, 65.6% ROE, and conservative 44.2% D/E, Lam combines elite profitability with a strong balance sheet [6].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on semiconductor equipment cyclicality — Lam's revenue declined 14% in FY2024 before recovering, demonstrating that even secular AI spending cannot fully insulate the business from fab investment cycles [6]. Forward P/E of 33.1x prices in sustained 20%+ growth, and the trailing P/E of 47x leaves limited room for execution misses or customer spending delays. China export restrictions remain a risk factor, with any tightening of semiconductor equipment export controls potentially impacting a significant revenue stream. CFO Bettinger's $20.5M in insider selling in March 2026 is notably large even by semiconductor industry standards [6]. WFE growth in 2026 is expected to be constrained by clean room space availability, creating a natural ceiling on near-term upside. The dividend yield of 0.46% provides minimal income support despite 9 consecutive years of growth.
key_catalysts: WFE spending projected at $135B in 2026 with advanced packaging growing >40%, driven by AI chip manufacturing demand across TSMC, Samsung, and Intel foundry expansions [1]
NAND recovery inflection beginning in 2026 adds a cyclical growth driver on top of the secular AI spending tailwind [1]
New COO Varadarajan appointment to 'increase company velocity for the AI era' signals organizational focus on capturing the accelerating semiconductor equipment opportunity [3]
key_risks: Semiconductor equipment cyclicality risk — FY2024's 14% revenue decline demonstrates vulnerability to fab investment cycle downturns despite secular AI tailwinds [6]
Forward P/E of 33.1x prices in sustained 20%+ growth; any deceleration from WFE spending constraints or customer delays would trigger significant multiple compression
China export control risk — tightening of semiconductor equipment restrictions could impact revenue, and geopolitical tensions create ongoing uncertainty for cross-border equipment sales [1]
